coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional amino functionalized graphene quantum dots wrapped upconversion nanoparticles for photodynamic therapy and X-ray CT imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Anjusha, A.J.
Thirunavukkarasu, S.
Resmi, A.N.
Jayasree, R.S.
Dhanapandian, S.
Krishnakumar, N.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we designed and developed a multifunctional nanocomposites by complexing NaGdF4:Yb/Er nanoparticles (UCNPs) with amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs). In the as-prepared nanocomposite (af-GQD/UCNPs), properties of UCNPs and af-GQDs were incorporated into a single nanoplatform to endow therapeutic and diagnostic functions. The UCNPs were employed as an imaging probe and a contrast agent for CT imaging, and the af-GQDs served as a therapeutic agent and photosensitizer (PS) by generating singlet oxygen for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Furthermore, the nanocomposites were investigated by electron microscopy, FTIR spectroscopy, zeta potential measurement, UV–vis spectroscopy, upconversion luminescence measurement, and cytotoxicity assessment. The in vitro experiments displayed excellent X-ray attenuation ability and PDT effects of af-GQD/UCNPs. Hence, the proposed multifunctional nanocomposites, which possesses upconversion luminescence, photodynamic, and X-ray attenuation properties, might be a viable option for application in bio-imaging and photodynamic therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Upconversion nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
Cytotoxicity
Bioimaging
Photodynamic therapy
X-ray CT imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of Yb3+/Er3+ doped NaGdF4 nanoparticles (OA-UCNPs)
Synthesis of af-GQDs modified NaGdF4:Yb/Er (af-GQDs/UCNPs) nanocomposites
Fluorescence imaging guided in vitro PDT

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of as-prepared af-GQDs with some marked with red circles; (b) UV–visible and PL spectra of af-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. The inset shows a photographic image of as-prepared water-soluble af-GQDs under UV light source. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
XRD patterns of (a) af-GQDs/UCNPs nanocomposites and (b) OA-UCNPs (JCPDS no. 27-0699 for pure hexagonal NaGdF4).
HR- TEM images of (a) OA-UCNPs in chloroform, (b) LF-UCNPs in water, and (c) af-GQDs/UCNPs in water (scale bar: 10 nm).
FTIR spectra of (a) OA-UCNPs, (b) LF-UCNPs, (c) af-GQDs and (d) af-GQDs/UCNPs nanocomposites.
The characteristic UV–vis absorption spectra of af-GQDs/UCNPs, af-GQDs and LF- UCNPs.
(a) Upconversion luminescence spectra, (b) visual fluorescence emission images under 975 nm excitations of OA-UCNPs, LF-UCNPs, and af-GQDs/UCNPs.
Time dependent decrease in DPBF absorption of (a) af-GQDs and (b) af-GQDs/UCNPs under 532 nm laser irradiation (0.1 W /cm2).

Cell viability of HeLa cells incubated with af-GQDs at different concentrations (a) without laser irradiation, (b) with laser irradiation, using a 532 nm laser, cell viability of HeLa cells incubated with af-GQDs/UCNPs at different concentrations (c) without laser irradiation, (d) with laser irradiation.
In vitro therapeutic evaluation of af-GQDs/UCNPs in HeLa cells in the absence and presence of laser (532 nm) through DCFDA analysis (where L and C4 represent light and af-GQDs/UCNPs, respectively).
(a) In vitro CT images of different concentrations of af-GQDs/UCNPs at room temperature (b) CT value of different concentrations of af-GQDs/UCNPs.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional amino functionalized graphene quantum dots wrapped upconversion nanoparticles for photodynamic therapy and X-ray CT imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Annamalai University, Annamalainagar, Tamil Nadu 608 002, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Biophotonics and Imaging, Department of Biomaterial Sciences and Technology, Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology (SCTIMST), Trivandrum 695012, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biomaterial Sciences and Technology
Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology (SCTIMST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Biophotonics and Imaging, Department of Biomaterial Sciences and Technology, Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology (SCTIMST), Trivandrum 695012, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, Palkalainagar, Madurai, Tamil Nadu 625 021, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics, Madurai Kamaraj University, Palkalainagar, Madurai, Tamil Nadu 625 021, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we designed and developed a multifunctional nanocomposites by complexing NaGdF4:Yb/Er nanoparticles (UCNPs) with amino functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs). In the as-prepared nanocomposite (af-GQD/UCNPs), properties of UCNPs and af-GQDs were incorporated into a single nanoplatform to endow therapeutic and diagnostic functions. The UCNPs were employed as an imaging probe and a contrast agent for CT imaging, and the af-GQDs served as a therapeutic agent and photosensitizer (PS) by generating singlet oxygen for photodynamic therapy (PDT). Furthermore, the nanocomposites were investigated by electron microscopy, FTIR spectroscopy, zeta potential measurement, UV–vis spectroscopy, upconversion luminescence measurement, and cytotoxicity assessment. The in vitro experiments displayed excellent X-ray attenuation ability and PDT effects of af-GQD/UCNPs. Hence, the proposed multifunctional nanocomposites, which possesses upconversion luminescence, photodynamic, and X-ray attenuation properties, might be a viable option for application in bio-imaging and photodynamic therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Various approaches have been proposed to date to achieve multi-functionality on a single theranostic nanoplatform [1]. Lanthanide doped upconversion luminescent materials are appealing because of their excellent photostability, lack of background fluorescence, narrow-band emission, long lifetime, absence of autofluorescence from biological tissues, and high penetration depth [2,3]. Upconversion nanomaterials belong to the class of fluorophores, which have the ability to convert low-energy photons to high-energy photons in UV/visible/NIR regions under excitation with a suitable NIR laser by the anti-Stokes mechanism [4]. Upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) have improved theranostic applications such as early cancer diagnosis and treatment due to their distinctive and appealing features [5]. Recently, significant advances have been made in tuning the optical properties of upconversion nanomaterials through upconversion-based nanocomposites, core–shell nanostructures, silica coated nanoparticles, UCNPs–magnetic nanoparticles (UCNPs–MNPs), etc. UC-based multifunctional nanocomposites, in particular, have great potential for biological applications such as bio-imaging, bio-sensors, drug delivery, and enhancing therapeutic effects with fewer side effects [6–9]. To improve the functionality of the nanomaterials, UC-based multifunctional nanocomposites integrate a special component at the nanoscale.

Among the fluorescent carbon materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a zero-dimensional nanomaterial with a size typically less than 20 nm, have become a research hotspot owing to their unique electrical, thermal, chemical, and optical properties. Recently, GQDs have reaped a lot of attention as multifunctional theranostic agents due to their enchanting characteristics, such as their large surface area, chemical inertness, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and resistance to photobleaching [10–13]. Due to the presence of rich carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, GQDs offer excellent water solubility and further functionalization with various organic or inorganic materials [14]. Moreover, the fluorescence effect of GQDs enables their wide range of applications as imaging probes and biosensors. So, graphene quantum dots modified UC nanocomposites offer a versatile nanoplatform for biomedical applications.

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a non-invasive clinical therapeutic practice that uses non-toxic light-sensitive agents called photosensitizers (PS) to destroy malignant and other diseased cells [13]. In a typical PDT process, first PS is injected into the patient; then the lesion is exposed to a suitable light source, leading to the generation of toxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) that cause cell death. When excited with a suitable wavelength of light, photosensitizers emit fluorescence, so PS can act as an imaging agent too. Consequently, simultaneous visualization and treatment of a lesion are possible with PDT [15]. So far, porphyrin-based molecules, zinc phthalocyanine, Ce6, hypocrellin A, methylene blue (MB), rhodamine B, rose Bengal (RB), etc. have been used as PS for fluorescence guided photodynamic therapy. Most of these conventional PSs suffer from poor water solubility, GQDs can improve the water solubility of the nanocarriers. Also, owing to the surface functionalization property GQDs can be modified with biocompatible molecules to enhance the selectivity and efficiency of PDT [1,16–23]. Recently, it was reported that GQDs can generate 1O2 upon excitation with suitable light, which suggests that GQDs can be promising photosensitizing agents for PDT [24,25]. In addition, several groups reported the utilization of GQDs for simultaneous imaging and enhanced PDT of cancer [26,27].

X-ray computed tomography (CT) is one of the most robust imaging tools in modern diagnostic medicine. They’ve received increasing attention owing to their high-resolution images with 3D structural details of tissues. Today, small iodinated molecules are mainly administered as contrast agents for X-ray CT imaging. Nanoparticles composed of heavy metal elements like gold, platinum, bismuth, and tantalum are an emerging new class of contrast agents. However, rare earth element-doped UCNPs are considered promising contrast agents for X-ray CT imaging since high atomic number elements bestow excellent X-ray absorption under clinical voltages [28–30]. In particular, Gd3+, Yb3+, and Lu3+ based UC materials displayed higher contrast efficiency due to the large k-edge energy and high X-ray mass attenuation coefficient [31].

In this paper, we propose a new idea for the preparation of graphene quantum dots modified NaGdF4:Yb/Er water soluble upconversion nanoparticles (af-GQD/UCNPs) and its applications in bio-imaging and photodynamic therapy of cancer cells. First, hydrophobic NaGdF4:Yb/Er nanoparticles were synthesized using a modified hydrothermal method as OA-UCNPs, and then the hydrophobic oleic acid ligands were eliminated by a facile acid treatment. Further, highly water soluble amino functionalized GQDs were prepared by a single step hydrothermal process. Then, amino functionalized GQDs were loaded onto the surface of the UCNPs by a simple stirring process. The positively charged acid treated UCNPs could easily interact with negatively charged af-GQDs through electrostatic force. The intrinsic properties of the new generation of 1O2 enable af-GQDs to serve as a photosensitizer (PS) for fluorescence guided PDT, while the UCNPs will be employed as a contrast agent for CT imaging. So, the optical properties, biocompatibility, PDT efficiency, and CT contrast potential of the as-synthesized nanomaterials have been studied in detail. The results demonstrated that the introduction of amino functionalized graphene quantum dots on the surface of the nanoparticles contributed to low toxicity and good water dispersibility without a significant decrease in UCL intensity. In addition, the dual functional nanocomposites offer simultaneous therapy and tracking of the status of the treated tumor lesion via CT imaging. Combining the properties of UCNPs and GQDs for potential imaging and therapy can have an impact on cancer treatment in the future.

In this work, all the reactants were AR-graded and used directly without any further purification. All reagent solutions were prepared in deionized water. Oleic acid (OA), ammonium fluoride (NH4F), Er2O3 (99.99%), Yb2O3 (99.99%), and Gd2O3 (99.99%), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), 2′,7′dichlorofluoresceinn diacetate (DCFH-DA), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ammonia (28 wt% in water) were bought from Merck. The chemicals used for the oxidation of graphite, including graphite powder, sodium nitrate, concentrated sulphuric acid (98%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%) were procured from SD Fine Chemicals, India. Absolute ethanol (99%), concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl), and chloroform has also been used in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of Yb3+/Er3+ doped NaGdF4 nanoparticles (OA-UCNPs)
ce:section.ce:para: A modified hydrothermal method was used to synthesize NaGdF4 doped with Yb3+ (18%) and Er3+ (2%). In a typical process, a 5 M solution of NaOH (6 ml) and 20 ml of ethanol were mixed. Further, 20 ml of oleic acid and 16 ml of a solution (0.1 M) of RECl3 (RE = Gd, Yb, and Er) were added to the above mixture under magnetic stirring. After that, 4 ml of 2 M NH4F was added and vigorously stirred for 30 min at room temperature. The mixture was then placed in a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The products were centrifuged and washed with a solution of ethanol and water thrice. Afterwards, the as-synthesized product was dried in a 60 °C vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of af-GQDs modified NaGdF4:Yb/Er (af-GQDs/UCNPs) nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Freshly prepared aqueous ligand free UCNPs (LF-UCNPs, 5 mg/ml) were mixed with a certain amount of amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots (af-GQDs) solution. Then, the mixture was stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Further, the obtained nanoparticles were centrifuged and washed three times with DI water. The resultant af-GQDs/ UCNPs were re-dispersed in water and stored for analysis.

The crystallinity of final products was analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (X’Pert Pro diffractometer) with Cu Kα radiation of wavelength 1.5418 Å. The surface functional groups before and after surface modification were studied using FTIR-ATR (Cary 630 FTIR, Agilent Technologies) spectra in the wavelength range of 3600 to 650 cm−1. The elemental compositions of UCNPs were investigated using EDS (FET Quanta FEG 200 spectrometer). Nanoparticle shape and size characterization were carried out using a HRTEM (Jeol/ JEM 2100). The optical absorption properties of the samples were evaluated by a UV–Vis spectrometer (Shimadzu – UV 1800) and upconversion luminescence spectra was recorded using a 975 nm NIR laser spectrophotometer (RI Instruments & Innovation India). PL spectra of the samples were acquired using a JobinYvon, FLUOROLOG-FL3-11 spectrofluorometer. Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were measured from Malvern ZS 90.

A chemical probe/trapping agent called DPBF was used to detect the production of singlet oxygen. In a typical experiment, af-GQDs/UCNPs dissolved in methanol and illuminated with a 532 nm laser beam at a power density of 0.1 W/cm2 for 2 min. Then, repeated the experiment with DPBF alone. Next, we mixed af-GQDs/UCNPs with 10 µL DPBF and subjected them to laser irradiation (532 nm, 0.1 W/cm2). The absorption of DPBF was monitored every 30 s and singlet oxygen generated from af-GQD/UCNPs was detected via the quenching of absorption of DPBF at 410 nm [32]. For comparison, the absorbance of af-GQDs and DPBF mixture was also recorded at the same experimental conditions.

Intracellular singlet oxygen generation of GQDs/UCNPs was observed using a cell permeant reagent DCFHDA in live cell samples. The cells, after two passages, were cultured in a 12 well dish for 24 h at 37 °C. After proper growth, the cells were treated with the af-GQDs/UCNPs with and without laser irradiation. After the treatment for 4 h and 30 min post laser irradiation, the cells were treated with 25 µL of DCFH-DA reagent and incubated for 40 min. The cells were then washed and added with PBS and images were taken using an inverted fluorescence microscope (IX83; Olympus Corp., Tokyo, Japan) with a cooled CCD camera (XM10, monochrome, Olympus).

CT imaging experiments were conducted using a 16-slice Aquilion Lightning CT imaging system with 80, 100, and 120 kV potential differences and 30 and 50 mA tube currents. To obtain CT phantom images of af-GQD/UCNPs, a series of aqueous solutions from 10 mg/ml to 1 mg/ml were prepared and assembled in 2 ml Eppendorf tubes. Then, CT values in Hounsfield units (HU) against each concentration were measured; water and GQDs solution were used as the controls from the specific regions of interest (ROI).Using the data, a graph of CT values at different concentrations was plotted to evaluate the efficacy of the nanoagents as contrast agents. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

Amino functionalized GQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal synthesis and studied using various analytic techniques to realize their properties. HR-TEM imaging revealed well distributed uniform af-GQDs with an average diameter ∼ 6 nm (Fig. 1 a). The optical properties of af-GQDs were characterized by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Fig. 1b depicts the combined spectra for GQDs optical absorbance and PL under variable excitation wavelengths. A typical broad absorption peak around 275 nm was observed, corresponding to π-π* transition of C = C within graphene, and an absorption peak at 325 nm representing the n- π* transition was also observed [33,34]. To analyze the PL behavior, af-GQDs were excited at wavelengths from 310 nm to 470 nm. Like other luminescent carbon materials,af-GQDs also exhibited excitation dependent PL behavior with strong emission maxima centered at 430 nm (λ ex: 340 nm). When the excitation was tuned from 310 nm to 470 nm the PL peak shifted to longer wavelengths and its intensity dropped remarkably. The inset of the figure shows the cyan fluorescence observed from af-GQDs under UV light exposure.

The as-synthesized af-GQDs were attached to the surface of the UCNPs through electrostatic interaction between the nanoparticles. Various characterization techniques were used to demonstrate the effect of nanoparticles coordination before and after modification. The crystallinity and phase of the af-GQDs/UCNPs and the effect of af-GQDs on the crystallinity of the UCNPs were examined by powder X-ray diffraction technique. As shown in Fig. 2 a and 2b, both the diffraction patterns of af-GQDs/UCNPs and OA-UCNPs are well-defined and matched well with the pure hexagonal structure of NaGdF4 (JCPDS no. 27-0699), indicating the highly crystalline nature of the nanocomposites. Furthermore, the absence of characteristic carbon diffraction peaks in Fig. 2a suggests that the incorporation of af-GQDs had a relatively minor effect on the crystallinity and phase of the β-NaGdF4 nanocrystals. Further, the morphology and size of the as-prepared nanostructures were revealed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM).HR-TEM images of OA-UCNPs, LF-UCNPs, and af-GQDs/UCNPs nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 3 (a), (b), and (c), respectively. From the TEM image, the average size distribution of OA-UCNPs was 30–40 nm and exhibited a clear hexagonal morphology. TEM micrographs of LF-UCNPs demonstrated highly crystalline, well-dispersed nanoparticles. Furthermore, the enlarged TEM image of af-GQDs/UCNPs illustrates hexagonal nanoparticles and af-GQDs coating the surfaces of the nanoparticles. Then, to assess the impact of surface modification, the surface charges of af-GQDs and af-GQDs/UCNPs were examined by zeta potential measurement. As shown in Fig. S1 (a), it is found that af-GQDs possess a negative charge ( - 37.5 mV), while the af-GQDs/UCNPs nanocomposite have a positive surface charge (2.32 mV). Besides, the DLS data of af-GQDs/UCNPs in Fig. S1(b) indicate that, hydrodynamic diameter of the sample was approximately 56.6 nm. The elemental composition of af-GQDs/UCNPs was validated using an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDS), and the presence of C, Na, F, Yb, Gd, O, and Er were confirmed (Fig. S2).

The effective combination of UCNPs and af-GQDs was evaluated using FTIR spectroscopy before and after coordination, as shown in Fig. 4 .The peaks of OA-UCNPs at 2926 cm−1 and 2857 cm−1 are attributed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the alkene group in the long alkyl chain of oleic acid. Besides, absorption peaks observed at 1556 cm−1 and 1451 cm−1 can be ascribed to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylic group in oleic acid (Fig. 4a) [35]. Moreover, a peak corresponding to C = O stretching vibration of carboxylic acid was observed at 1713 cm−1. After acid treatment, the intensities of oleic acid-related absorption bands were greatly reduced, and a strong absorption peak corresponding to O–H absorption was observed at 1645 cm−1 (Fig. 4b). The results indicated that, the ligand removal of oleic acid from the surface of nanoparticles has been successfully accomplished [36]. In the FTIR spectra of amino functionalized GQDs (af-GQDs),a broad absorbance appeared in the range of 3600 - 3000 cm−1 has been assigned to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H groups (Fig. 4c). Additionally, some peaks representing C-N stretching vibrations, N–H bending of primary and secondary amines were also detected at 1365 cm−1, 1524 cm−1 and 1638 cm−1, respectively [1,34,37–39]. Thus, the results confirmed the functionalization of amino groups with GQDs. From Fig. 4d, the successful combination of UCNPs with af-GQDS was evidenced by the peaks at 1636 cm−1, 1508 cm−1 and 1377 cm−1 compared to the absorptions of af-GQDS and UCNPs. The above results confirmed that af-GQDS was auspiciously attached to the surface of the UCNPs.

UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and upconversion luminescence analysis were used to analyze the optical properties of the synthesized nanomaterials. First, the absorbance spectra of LF-UCNPs, af-GQDs, and af-GQDs/UCNPs were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.As shown in Fig. 5 , af-GQDs absorb at two distinct wavelengths: a broad absorption around 275 nm and a peak at 325 nm. The absorbance spectra of LF-UCNPs exhibited no visible peaks in the UV–vis region. Meanwhile, an absorption of around 275 nm was observed in the af-GQDs/UCNPs nanocomposites, which was attributed to the incorporation of GQDs on the surface of the UCNPs. So, by comparing the optical absorption spectra, it is confirmed that the af-GQDs/UCNPs nanocomposites was successfully synthesized.

Fig. 6 a shows the room temperature upconversion luminescence (UCL) spectra recorded for OA-UCNPs, LF-UCNPs, and af-GQDs/UCNPs under 975 nm CW laser excitation. Four major characteristic bands representing energy transfer between Yb3+ and Er3+ were clearly resolved in the spectra, with maxima at ∼ 408 nm, ∼ 523 nm, ∼555 nm and ∼ 656 nm, respectively. The blue emission band was assigned to the 2H9/2 to 4I15/2 electron transition of Er3+. The green UCL at 523 nm and 555 nm can be attributed to the 2 H11/2 and 4S3/2 to 4 I15/2 transitions of Er3+respectively, while the intensified red emission can be attributed to the 4F9/2 to 4I15/2 transition [40,41]. Due to the presence of oleic acid coating, the UCL of OA-UCNPs was dramatically enhanced. Meanwhile, the luminescence intensity of oleic acid stripped nanoparticles (LF-UCNPs) was slightly reduced. The nanocomposites sample showed a quenching in UCL intensity after conjugation with the af-GQDs. Fig. 6(b) shows the visual emission images of OA-UCNPs, LF-UCNPs, and af-GQDs/UCNPs samples illuminated with a 975 nm NIR laser. As exhibited in the figure, the brightness of green light was diminished in LF-UCNPs due to the removal of the oleic acid coating from the surface of OA-UCNPs. Owing to the addition of af-GQDs, the brightness of green light was further weakened in the af-GQDs/UCNPs nanocomposite. In view of the above observations, af-GQDs won't change the energy transition of the UCNPs, so af-GQDs/UCNPs are suitable for various optical probe applications.

Ge et al. demonstrated that GQDs are able to produce 1O2 upon suitable light illumination via a multistate excitation process [25]. To apply the as-prepared nanocomposites as a PDT agent, singlet oxygen generation was observed using UV–Vis spectroscopy. Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was employed as a chemical probe to track the singlet oxygen production during predetermined irradiation times. The time-dependent decay in DPBF absorption of af-GQDs and af-GQDs/UCNPs in methanol irradiated with a 532 nm laser (0.1 W/cm2) was plotted. Then, the decay in the absorption of DPBF at 410 nm, which indicates the presence of 1O2 was analyzed for different time intervals. Fig. 7 shows the absorbance intensity of nanoformulations of af-GQDs and af-GQDs/UCNPs as a function of laser irradiation time. The absorbance intensity of the DPBF mixed solution diminished continuously during the laser irradiation for both af-GQDs and af-GQDs/UCNPs. The results indicate that the prepared af-GQDs/UCNPs can be used as an efficient agent for PDT.

An MTT assay has been conducted to evaluate the photo-induced toxicity profile of af-GQDs and af-GQDs/UCNPs nanocomposites (supplementary material). Human cervical cancer cell lines (HeLa) were selected as the control cell lines to investigate the laser triggered PDT effect of the nanomaterials using the MTT assay. HeLa cells were irradiated with a 532 nm laser (0.1 W/cm2) for 1 min after the uptake of nanomaterials. Results showed that af-GQDs have a negligible effect on cell viability without laser irradiation, even at high concentrations (500 μg/mL) (Fig. 8 (a)). As shown in Fig. 8 (b), af-GQDs displayed cytotoxicity and dose-dependent cell death under photoirradiation. As seen from Fig. 8 (c) without the irradiation of lasers, cell death is negligible at all tested concentrations (50 μg/ml to 700 μg/ml). After 1 min of irradiation, the cell viability suddenly falls to ∼ 60% (50 μg/ml), and a dose-dependent cytotoxicity was observed due to the presence of singlet oxygen (Fig. 8(d)). Moreover, at the highest concentration (700 μg/ml) cellular viability significantly dropped to ∼ 40%. So, irradiation resulted in greater cytotoxicity for af-GQDs/UCNPs nanoparticles compared to graphene quantum dots; therefore the as-prepared nanocomposites can be employed as a PDT agent for enhanced and efficacious cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging guided in vitro PDT
ce:section.ce:para: The DCFHDA reagent was selected as a fluorescent probe for the identification of intracellular singlet oxygen generation. DCFHDA enters the cells through diffusion and converts into the non-fluorescent material DCFH. Later, DCFH reacts with ROS and oxidizes into highly fluorescent dichlorofluorescein(DCF). DCF can be detected by fluorescent spectroscopy with 485 nm excitation, resulting in bright green fluorescence at 535 nm [42]. Hoechst, a blue fluorescent dye, was used to stain the DNA/nucleus of the cells. Here, human cervical cancer cell lines (HeLa) were selected to conduct the PDT experiments. As presented in Fig. 9 , the blue fluorescent portion represents the live HeLa cells stained with Hoechst. Further, the HeLa cells were irradiated with a 532 nm laser (0.1 W/cm2) for 1 min to assess the light-driven ROS production before and after treatment with af-GQDs/UCNPs nanocomposite. After irradiation, the untreated cells showed no green fluorescence, but the cells treated with nanocomposites exhibited a brilliant green luminescence. Furthermore, the merged image of the experiments demonstrated the production of a remarkably high level of ROS in HeLa cells. The results indicate that af-GQDs/UCNPs, in the presence of light, can generate toxic ROS, so that they can be used to induce light-triggered ablation of cancer cells, or the PDT effect.

Rare earth element-based nanoparticles outperform currently used functional iodinated clinical CT contrast agents in terms of contrast performance due to their high X-ray attenuation characteristics [43]. Because of the presence of strong X-ray-absorbing rare earth metals in these nanoparticles, such as Gd (Z = 64) and Yb (Z = 70) GQDs and modified Yb/Er co-doped NaGdF4 (af-GQD/UCNPs), they can be used as contrast agents in X-ray and CT imaging. Hence, in order to analyze the same, various concentrations of aqueous af-GQD/UCNPs were prepared and scanned in a whole-body 16-slice Aquilion Lightning CT imaging system. From Fig. 10 (a), it is clearly seen that the X-ray attenuation has significantly improved with the increase in af-GQD/UCNPs concentrations (10 mg/ml to 1 mg/ml). Further, the CT numbers, or Hounsfield units (HU), were measured and plotted against the GQD/UCNPs concentrations. From the graph (Fig. 10 (b)), a well-correlated linear relationship was observed with a slope of 19.01. Thus, the in vitro CT imaging results propose that af-GQD/UCNPs hold great potential as a nanoprobe for medical imaging applications.

In summary, we prepared an aminofunctionalized GQDs-based upconversion nanocomposites (af-GQDs/UCNPs) and subsequently characterized its properties using various techniques. UCNPs assist the system in enabling X-ray attenuation properties, and GQDs serve as a photosensitizer to generate singlet oxygen under photoirradiation with a suitable light source. The as-prepared nanoparticles displayed excellent photoluminescence even after modification with graphene quantum dots. The nanoparticles were biocompatible and non-toxic in the dark, whereas laser irradiation could initiate a photodynamic effect, resulting in tumor suppression. In addition, CT phantom studies of af-GQDs/UCNPs have revealed their outstanding contrast potential as an imaging probe in biomedical applications. So, the collective properties of the components of af-GQDs/UCNPs enable them to function as a multifunctional nanoplatform for simultaneous high-resolution imaging and photo-guided treatment of cancer. Based on observations, this work can lead to further modifications of the nanocomposite surface with specific biocompatible molecules, ligands, or coatings for future new nanoplatform for bio-imaging and anti-cancer therapy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere thanks to DST-PURSE (PHASE II) for providing fine chemicals to carry out a part of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.110428.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
